summary:
Durrington Walls, located near Amesbury in Wiltshire approximately 3 kilometres northeast of [[event:43|Stonehenge]], was one of the largest Neolithic settlements in Britain, occupied around 2500–2460 BC. Archaeological excavations, particularly those conducted as part of the Stonehenge Riverside Project in the 2000s, revealed evidence of a large circular timber enclosure surrounded by a massive henge (bank and ditch), with associated avenues and a settlement of substantial timber houses that may have housed hundreds or thousands of people during seasonal gatherings.

Durrington Walls appears to have functioned as the living counterpart to Stonehenge's monument for the dead. While Stonehenge is associated with cremation burials and the permanent stone monuments of the ancestors, the evidence from Durrington Walls — massive quantities of animal bones indicating large-scale feasting, and the remains of many temporary structures — suggests it was a place of seasonal celebration and communal gathering for the living. The settlement was likely occupied around the winter solstice, when large numbers of people came to feast and participate in rituals associated with the construction and use of Stonehenge.

The Southern Circle at Durrington Walls, a large circular arrangement of timber posts within the henge, is interpreted as a roofed building of considerable size used for communal activities. Analysis of animal bones from the site indicates that cattle and pigs were brought from distant regions of Britain to be slaughtered and consumed at feasts, suggesting that Durrington Walls attracted people from a very wide catchment area. The site thus provides crucial evidence for understanding the social organisation, ritual life, and long-distance connections of the people who built Stonehenge.
